content.problemStatement:
For a permutation PPP, we define f(P)f(P)f(P) to be it's prefix max array, i.e. f(P)i=max‚Å°(P1,P2,‚Ä¶,Pi)f(P)_i = \max(P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_i)f(P)i‚Äã=max(P1‚Äã,P2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,Pi‚Äã).

Now, you are given a partially filled in permutation PPP of the integers [1,N][1, N][1,N], i.e. some elements are ‚àí1-1‚àí1. You want to replace the ‚àí1-1‚àí1 elements with integers in [1,N][1, N][1,N] to make it a permutation.

What are the total number of distinct f(P)f(P)f(P) possible? Since the answer may be large, print it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line contains a single integer NNN.
The second line contains NNN integers - P1,P2,‚Ä¶,PNP_1, P_2, \ldots, P_NP1‚Äã,P2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,PN‚Äã.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the number of possible values of f(P)f(P)f(P) modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints: All the non ‚àí1-1‚àí1 values in PPP are distinct.
The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 500050005000.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1 : All 666 permutations of N=3N = 3N=3 are possible. But [3,1,2][3, 1, 2][3,1,2] and [3,2,1][3, 2, 1][3,2,1] form the same prefix maximum array, and hence there are only 555 distinct prefix maximum arrays possible.
